one_line_takeaway: RAGuard is a lightweight, training-free RAG defense that expands retrieval and applies percentile-based perplexity and query-similarity filters to strip poisoned texts, achieving ~92-97% detection accuracy and near-100% output accuracy under PoisonedRAG while keeping false positives under ~6%.
key_finding: RAGuard substantially outperforms perplexity-based detectors and both detection- and prevention-based baselines. Under the strongest attack (PoisonedRAG) it reaches DACC of 96.2% (NQ), 92.4% (MS-MARCO), 94.1% (HotpotQA), 96.8% (ENQ), 92.4% (EMS-MARCO) with OACC near 100%, while keeping FPR typically below 6%. It beats PPL by ~30% DACC and PPL-window by ~20% DACC, and gives ~15% higher OACC than both. It stays effective against both tailored adaptive attacks (e.g., DACC 0.923-0.983 across datasets) and adds only slight runtime overhead over plain RAG.
core_contribution: The paper introduces RAGuard, a non-parametric, training-free detection framework that removes poisoned texts from a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) knowledge database at inference time. Its own contribution has three coupled components: (1) retrieval expansion, retrieving top-N texts with N=3k instead of the usual top-k to dilute the fraction of poisoned content in the candidate pool; (2) a two-stage filter that classifies each retrieved text as poisoned via empirical-percentile hypothesis tests on three scores computed against a random reference sample S of the database: a chunk-wise Perplexity Difference (PD, splitting each text into two chunks and measuring the perplexity gap between them), a Perplexity Maximum (PM, the higher of the two chunk perplexities), and a query-Text-Similarity score (TS, flagging texts abnormally similar to the query, motivated by the fact that HotFlip-style attacks push injected texts to high query similarity); a text is dropped if ANY of the three percentile tests trips. The paper also contributes a formal Theorem 1 giving a lower bound OACC >= 1 - exp(-ck) on output accuracy when the effective surviving poisoned fraction rho*beta_total < 1/2 (proved via a Chernoff bound in Appendix A), and it designs two tailored adaptive attacks (GPT-4 paraphrasing; human rewriting) to stress-test RAGuard. NOTE: TrustRAG (K-means clustering with cosine+ROUGE), PoisonedRAG, RobustRAG, paraphrasing, duplicate-hash filtering, and the PPL/PPL-window detectors are cited prior work used as baselines/attacks, NOT RAGuard's own contribution.
threat_model: Data/knowledge poisoning against RAG. The attacker injects multiple crafted poisoned texts (5 per target query by default, following PoisonedRAG) into the knowledge database so that when a specific target query is issued, the poisoned texts are retrieved in Phase I and steer the LLM toward an attacker-chosen answer. Adopting the PoisonedRAG threat model, the attacker cannot read the existing database contents, cannot query the LLM, and does not know the LLM's parameters. The paper focuses on the stronger white-box-retriever setting (attacker knows retrieval parameters). The defender has no prior knowledge of the attack method or the number of poisoned texts. Two adaptive attackers who know how RAGuard filters (and try to make poisoned texts look benign) are also considered.
limitations: OACC degrades once poisoned texts exceed ~7 per query, because most queries have <=5 related clean texts in the database (the authors argue such heavy injection is impractical/detectable). The default evaluation assumes only 5 poisoned texts per query. Results depend on a benign reference sample S drawn from the same (already-poisoned) database, and the perplexity-LLM matters (GPT-2 > Bloomz-560M > Facebook-1.3B). Evaluation is restricted to close-ended target queries only (no open-ended questions), reuses the PoisonedRAG 100-query setup, and Theorem 1's guarantee rests on assumed independence of the three filters and treats the LLM as answering correctly whenever a majority of the k texts are benign. The TS filter's core assumption (poisoned texts have abnormally high query similarity) is exactly what a similarity-aware adaptive attacker would target.

quantitative_results: PoisonedRAG attack, RAGuard DACC: 96.2% NQ, 92.4% MS-MARCO, 94.1% HotpotQA, 96.8% ENQ, 92.4% EMS-MARCO; OACC near 100% on all
RAGuard outperforms PPL by ~30% DACC and PPL-window by ~20% DACC, with ~15% higher OACC than both
RAGuard FPR typically below 6% across datasets
PoisonedRAG OACC (Table VI): No defense 0.000, Paraphrasing 0.175 (NQ)/0.294 (MS-MARCO)/0.231 (HotpotQA), Duplicate 0.132/0.206/0.167, RobustRAG 0.632/0.574/0.530, RAGuard 0.999/0.973/0.988
TrustRAG (Table V) achieves high DACC but very high FPR ~0.52-0.74 (e.g., NQ PoisonedRAG DACC 0.941 but FPR 0.552, OACC only 0.562)
Adaptive attacks (Table VIII): RAGuard DACC 0.923-0.957 (Adaptive I) and 0.977-0.992 (Adaptive II), OACC 0.970-0.999
Component ablation (Table VII, PoisonedRAG): full RAGuard beats all five reduced variants; e.g., on MS-MARCO Variant II (similarity-only) DACC 0.202 vs full 0.924
Perplexity-LLM effect: GPT-2 best (e.g., NQ PoisonedRAG DACC 0.962), Bloomz-560M 0.951, Facebook-1.3B 0.934
OACC begins to decline when poisoned texts per query exceed 7; most queries have <=5 related clean texts
Theorem 1 guarantee: OACC >= 1 - exp(-ck) with c = (1/3)(1/2 - rho*beta_total)^2 * rho*beta_total when rho*beta_total < 1/2
baselines_compared: TrustRAG (K-means clustering with cosine + ROUGE, advanced detection-based)
Attacks used: Prompt injection, General trigger (Phantom), Jamming, PoisonedRAG, plus 2 tailored adaptive attacks
